# Transcrição: 03-Turnaround King Deals

- **Fonte:** 03-Turnaround King Deals
- **Duração:** 04:53

## [00:00 - 03:01]

You might have remembered that Grant Cardone had a TV show called The Turn-Around King on National Geographic. On the show, he'd go into distressed businesses and turn them around. Those are the exact type of businesses I want to highlight in this lesson. Turn-Around King type of deals. Or what are commonly referred to as distressed deals. We're going to go more into the details as we go through this course. Distressed deals are businesses or business owners that are in dire need of help. In these cases, the business in question could be on death row. They could be struggling barely able to survive. Or it could be something like a death in the family and they don't have an operator or owner to run the business. Because of the stresses of the business, the seller is totally overwhelmed. He or she could be strapped for cash with only 30 or 60 days of money in the bank. Maybe there's no operator to run the business because of a death in the family. Depending on what your area of expertise is as a business buyer, these types of deals could be perfect for you. The nice thing about these deals are depending on how distressed the business is, you might be able to close on the deal relatively quickly. It all comes down to showing up to your meeting with the seller and positioning yourself as the turnaround king like Grant was on his TV show. Oftentimes, the seller is more than happy to part with their business, which they really view as a problem at this point, a weight in their life. Because you're providing them with an immediate solution and relief, they'll be ready to go. You show up to the meeting, have a conversation and position yourself as their turnaround king. There are benefits to this deal type in terms of finding deals too. You can get distressed deal flow using additional sources like credit agencies, etc. We'll talk about some of these things a little later. Because the seller is in this situation, it's relatively easy to set up meetings to discuss acquiring them. You just have to make it known you specialize in turning around businesses and you'd like to discuss their business with them. And properly, you'll be their knight in shining armor. Sometimes these businesses can literally be bought for a dollar just by taking over the liabilities and taking on the employees. But there are risks too. It takes work to turn around a failing business. Getting a yes on a distress deal often doesn't take that much. It just takes a little patience with a distressed seller so you can help them through the process. If they're ready to get out of the business, they'll be thrilled that you're there to take it on. When you're doing this, remember there's an emotional pain with the seller.

## [03:01 - 04:53]

So giving them a little bit of peace of mind that you're going to take that business and make it work. Especially when there's employees and customers involved is very, very important. This is going to be easier selling it to you than shutting the business down themselves. You're also offering the seller the chance to save face. To be clear, you're not looking for insolvent or bankrupt businesses. Those are especially hard to turn around. Instead, you're looking for businesses headed in that direction but not past the point of no return. We'll cover how to know what point of no return is later on. Before that, I want to cover the last benefit of a distress deal. There is a safety net, so to speak, if you're unable to get the business working as fast as you'd hope. Now, if you acquire a distress business, there is some golden gems in that business which is shutting down areas of the business that are losing money and focusing on areas of the business that make money. In fact, there's a special purpose vehicle known as an SPV. SPVs are defined as a subsidiary company that is formed to undertake a specific business purpose or activity. SPVs are commonly utilized in certain structured finance applications. With this strategy, you will use one of the SPVs you've created to acquire the distress business and its original asset. If any of this sounds too complex right now, don't worry. I'm just trying to open your eyes to all the tools, strategies, and opportunities there are when acquiring a business. And as we go through the rest of this course, you'll become more and more confident in your decision making. Next up, we'll cover some of my favorite deal types for newbie acquisition entrepreneurs.
